steps.title: Separate the clauses
steps.content: The first clause says that the sand at Balos Beach is a more unusual shade. The next clause explains the cause: deposits of crushed coral and other organic matter lend the sand a pink hue. The word "though" works as a transition after the first clause, so the punctuation must separate the two independent clauses.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A - shade, though: This makes "though deposits of crushed coral and other organic matter lend the sand a unique pink hue" a dependent clause, but the sentence needs a separate independent clause that explains the unusual shade.
  • Choice B - shade: though: A colon does not correctly introduce the transition "though" before the next clause.
  • Choice C - shade, though,: The comma after "though" leaves only a comma between the two independent clauses.
  • Choice D - shade, though;: The comma places "though" after the first independent clause, and the semicolon separates that clause from the next independent clause.
The answer is D.
